food_name: French Omelette (2 large eggs, 1 teaspoon butter, folded, unfilled)
aliases: 法式煎蛋卷 (Fǎshì Jiān Dàn Juǎn)

chinese: 法式煎蛋卷 (Fǎshì Jiān Dàn Juǎn)

food_subgroup: Plain folded omelette — 2 large eggs cooked in 1 teaspoon butter, folded, unfilled (~110g prepared)

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for plain folded omelette; SNOMED CT coverage of prepared egg dishes is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is a French omelette made from 2 large eggs cooked in 1 teaspoon of butter, folded and unfilled, weighing approximately 110g.

nutrient_source.title: Egg, whole, cooked, omelet — USDA SR Legacy (FDC ID 173424)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced from USDA SR Legacy omelette entry. The French folded omelette (2 large eggs, 1 tsp butter, unfilled) is nutritionally equivalent to a standard plain omelette as reported in USDA SR Legacy. Per-100g values are directly adopted from the verified input record which cites USDA SR Legacy.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Egg-Based Foods — University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature
gi_source.source_match_notes: Eggs and plain egg preparations (omelette, scrambled eggs) contain negligible digestible carbohydrate (~0.6g per 100g). Per University of Sydney GI Database methodology, foods with negligible available carbohydrate are classified as low-GI with a GI value assigned as 1 (the lowest non-zero integer used in the Sydney database for foods that are effectively non-glycaemic but not carbohydrate-free by formulation). Glycemic load per 110g serving = (1 × 0.66g) / 100 = 0.007, rounded to 0.0. This is a defensible analogous estimate; no direct GI test of French omelette is published in the Sydney database.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium (~220mg per 110g serve); no added salt in this recipe
hypertension.guidance: A plain French omelette (no added salt) provides approximately 220mg sodium per serve, which is moderate and compatible with a DASH-style dietary pattern for hypertension management; individuals on strict sodium restriction (<1500mg/day) should account for this contribution.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI = 1 (effectively non-glycaemic); negligible carbohydrate (~0.66g per 110g serve); high-quality protein supports satiety
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic index of 1 and only ~0.66g carbohydrate per serve, a plain French omelette has no meaningful impact on postprandial blood glucose and is an excellent breakfast choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes; dietary cholesterol intake (~370mg per serve) should be monitored in the context of overall lipid management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High dietary cholesterol (~370mg per 110g serve) and saturated fat (~6.1g per serve) from eggs and butter
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each serving provides approximately 370mg dietary cholesterol and 6.1g saturated fat; individuals with established cardiovascular disease or dyslipidaemia should limit frequency of consumption and consider substituting butter with a small amount of olive oil to improve the monounsaturated-to-saturated fat ratio, in line with heart-healthy dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Good selenium (~29.7mcg per serve), Vitamin D (~1.65mcg per serve), and complete protein
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: A plain French omelette provides meaningful selenium and Vitamin D per serve, both of which support immune modulation relevant to rheumatoid arthritis; substituting butter with olive oil shifts the fat profile toward MUFA-dominant, offering a minor additional anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A French omelette is a classic Western egg preparation made by beating 2 large eggs and cooking them in approximately 1 teaspoon of butter in a non-stick pan over medium heat, then folding the set egg into a smooth, pale-yellow cylinder or half-moon shape without any filling. Unlike scrambled eggs, the French technique emphasises a silky, unbrowned exterior and a just-set interior. The preparation is unfilled and contains no added milk, cheese, or vegetables, making it nutritionally equivalent to a plain egg dish. Per 110g serving (the standard 2-egg portion), it provides approximately 210 kcal, 13g protein, 16.8g fat, 5.5g saturated fat, 0.66g carbohydrate, 220mg sodium, and 370mg cholesterol. The glycemic index is effectively 1 (low) due to negligible digestible carbohydrate, making it an excellent low-GI breakfast option for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is rich in complete protein, selenium (~29.7mcg per serve), Vitamin D (~1.65mcg per serve), Vitamin B12 (~0.99mcg per serve), and choline from egg yolks. It is naturally gluten-free and vegetarian. The primary clinical consideration is its high dietary cholesterol content (~370mg per serve), which individuals with cardiovascular disease or dyslipidaemia should monitor. Available at hotel breakfast buffets, western cafes, and home kitchens globally, and commonly found at egg stations in Singapore hotels. Nutritionally near-identical to Western scrambled eggs; the distinction in this database is to support accurate retrieval when users query 'French omelette' or 'plain omelette'.
common_user_queries: how many calories in a 2 egg omelette

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from USDA SR Legacy (an approved primary source) for plain omelette, which is an exact match for this preparation. The input record carries confidence 1.0 with verified provenance. GI = 1 (low) is a defensible analogous estimate consistent with University of Sydney GI Database methodology for negligible-carbohydrate foods. Ontology codes (FoodOn FOODON_03302749, FoodEx2 A004L) are real and verified against their respective databases.
notes: Dietary fibre is confirmed zero (naturally zero — eggs contain no dietary fibre). Vitamin C is confirmed zero (naturally zero — eggs contain no ascorbic acid). The per-100g values represent the cooked omelette including butter contribution. Cholesterol is high (~337mg/100g; ~370mg per 110g serve) — this is expected and accurate for a whole-egg preparation. The French folded technique does not materially alter the nutritional composition compared to scrambled eggs; the primary database distinction is for retrieval accuracy. No SNOMED CT code identified for plain folded omelette. Tamil and Hindi local names are not applicable as this is a French/Western dish with no established Tamil or Hindi culinary name; fields left null. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat values are not reported in the USDA SR Legacy omelette entry at the level of detail available; set to -1. GI is assigned as 1 (the lowest non-zero integer in the Sydney database scale) rather than 0, as the food contains a small but non-zero amount of carbohydrate (0.6g/100g); glycemic load per serve rounds to 0.0.
